questions.question: Explain the difference between flow control and congestion control in TCP.
keyPoints: Flow control is end-to-end, prevents receiver overwhelm using sliding window
Congestion control is network-wide, prevents network overwhelm using cwnd
Flow control uses receiver's advertised window
Congestion control uses network feedback (timeouts, duplicate ACKs)

questions.question: Describe the four phases of TCP congestion control with a brief explanation of each.
keyPoints: Slow Start: cwnd starts at 1 MSS, doubles every RTT until ssthresh
Congestion Avoidance: cwnd increases linearly (1/cwnd per ACK) after reaching ssthresh
Fast Retransmit: Retransmit on 3 duplicate ACKs without waiting for timeout
Fast Recovery: Continue sending after fast retransmit, reduce cwnd to ssthresh/2

questions.question: What is Random Early Detection (RED) algorithm? Explain its working with the three threshold parameters.
keyPoints: RED is Active Queue Management (AQM) technique
minthresh: below this, no drops occur
maxthresh: above this, all packets are dropped
Between thresholds: drop probability increases linearly with queue length
Purpose: provide early congestion notification to senders

questions.question: Differentiate between Leaky Bucket and Token Bucket algorithms for traffic shaping.
keyPoints: Leaky Bucket: outputs at constant rate, converts bursty to uniform flow
Token Bucket: allows bursts up to bucket capacity, enforces average rate
Leaky Bucket: no burst tolerance - excess traffic dropped
Token Bucket: burst tolerance - can accumulate tokens for larger bursts
Both used for traffic policing and shaping

questions.question: Explain AIMD (Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease) algorithm used in TCP congestion control.
keyPoints: AIMD is the fundamental principle of TCP congestion control
Additive Increase: during normal operation, cwnd increases linearly (1 MSS per RTT)
Multiplicative Decrease: when congestion detected, cwnd is halved
This sawtooth pattern ensures fair bandwidth sharing and network stability
Converges to optimal operating point over time

questions.question: What is congestion collapse? What are the main causes and how can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Congestion collapse: excessive unnecessary packets in network causing severe degradation
Causes: packet retransmissions without awareness of dropped packets, feedback delays
Prevention: proper congestion control algorithms, timeout calculations, limit retransmissions
TCP's congestion control mechanisms help prevent this

questions.question: Explain the working of TCP Reno with respect to Fast Recovery mechanism.
keyPoints: TCP Reno builds upon TCP Tahoe by adding Fast Recovery
When 3 duplicate ACKs received: set ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = ssthresh + 3*MSS
Perform Fast Retransmit for missing segment
Enter Fast Recovery phase: continue sending new data with reduced cwnd
After recovery, enter Congestion Avoidance with new cwnd

questions.question: Describe in detail the TCP congestion control algorithm. Explain how Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery work together with appropriate diagrams and cwnd behavior.
keyPoints: Initial cwnd = 1 MSS, ssthresh = 65535 bytes
Slow Start: cwnd doubles every RTT (increases by 1 MSS per ACK)
When cwnd >= ssthresh, enter Congestion Avoidance: cwnd increases by 1 per RTT
On timeout: ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = 1, restart Slow Start
On 3 duplicate ACKs: Fast Retransmit, set ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = ssthresh + 3, Fast Recovery
After Fast Recovery, enter Congestion Avoidance with new cwnd
Diagram showing sawtooth pattern of cwnd

questions.question: Explain the Token Bucket algorithm for traffic shaping. How does it differ from Leaky Bucket? Provide suitable diagrams and applications of both algorithms.
keyPoints: Token Bucket: tokens added at constant rate r, bucket capacity b max tokens
To send packet of size n, n tokens must be consumed
If insufficient tokens, packet waits or is dropped
Allows bursts up to bucket capacity (b tokens)
Leaky Bucket: constant output rate regardless of input
No burst tolerance - excess input dropped or queued
Token Bucket used for traffic shaping with burst allowance
Leaky Bucket used for strict rate limiting
Applications: VoIP traffic policing, network traffic management, QoS

questions.question: Explain the Random Early Detection (RED) algorithm in detail. How does it provide early congestion notification? Compare it with traditional drop-tail queue management.
keyPoints: RED is Active Queue Management algorithm
Calculates average queue size using exponential weighted moving average
Three parameters: minthresh, maxthresh, maxP
If avg < minthresh: no drops
If minthresh < avg < maxthresh: drop probability = maxP × (avg - minthresh)/(maxthresh - minthresh)
If avg > maxthresh: drop all packets
Advantages over drop-tail: prevents global synchronization, early notification, reduced average queueing delay
Disadvantages: parameter sensitivity, may not work well with bursty traffic

questions.question: Compare TCP Tahoe, TCP Reno, and TCP NewReno congestion control algorithms. Explain their differences in handling packet loss and recovery.
keyPoints: TCP Tahoe: Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit; on loss: cwnd = 1, ssthresh = cwnd/2, restart Slow Start
TCP Reno: Adds Fast Recovery; on 3 duplicate ACKs: cwnd = ssthresh + 3, fast retransmit, continue sending; on timeout: same as Tahoe
TCP NewReno: Improves Reno to handle multiple packet losses in one window; enters Fast Recovery until all losses acknowledged
NewReno distinguishes between partial ACKs and full ACKs during recovery
Performance: NewReno > Reno > Tahoe under multiple loss scenarios
All use AIMD principle for congestion control
